role_summary: Albany, Georgia public-school teacher and Albany Movement participant; author of the 2000 pictorial history Open Dem Cells (Brentwood Academic Press); a founding contributor to the Albany Civil Rights Institute at Old Mt. Zion Baptist Church.
ai_reading: Jenkins's most frequent semantic neighbors across her 43 passages are Mary Jones (entry 85) and the West-Coast Black children's-book author Mildred Pitts Walter (entry 91), tied at fourteen co-retrievals each, followed by a three-way tie at eight: Johnnie Ruth McCullar (entry 65), William G. Anderson (entry 126), and Lisa Anderson Todd (entry 79). The single closest passage by cosine is Mary Jones at 0.88, which is the shared Albany interview-day boilerplate (both interviews were recorded in Albany on March 9, 2013), so the strongest raw signal is a recording-session artifact rather than a thematic match. What is striking is that the cluster does not concentrate on one movement organization (SNCC, SCLC, NAACP) the way most organizer entries do; the embedding reads Jenkins as a teacher-and-documentarian voice whose nearest neighbors mix a children's-book author, two other Albany interviewees, and William G. Anderson, the osteopathic physician who served as the Albany Movement's first president. On the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis her normalized position is +0.83, the second-most-individual reading in the entire 136-entry corpus; only Grace Hall Miller (entry 50, +1.00) sits further at the Individual Conscience pole. The placement tracks the texture of her own testimony, which is autobiographical and personal throughout: the water pistol and her mother's insistence that she could not give chase, the salutatorian's nerves, the white supervisor's parting line that ended her time in Baker County, dialing a phone in King's borrowed office. The embedding reads that first-person register of conscience and lived episode rather than the committee-and-campaign vocabulary that gives the SNCC and SCLC organizer entries their Collective Discipline weighting, even though Jenkins was herself an active office volunteer and note-taker inside the Movement's machinery.
biographical_paragraph: Mary Frances Jenkins was born in Waycross, Georgia on August 12, 1928, and moved to Albany around age eight after her mother remarried [src: 1]. She graduated as salutatorian of Madison High School in Albany, attended Fisk University in Nashville, and returned to teach, first at East Baker High School in neighboring Baker County and then at Carver Junior High School in Albany [src: 1]. She joined the Albany Movement after attending its first mass meeting at Mt. Zion Baptist Church, and volunteered in the office set up in the home of William G. Anderson (entry 126), the osteopathic physician who served as the Movement's first president, when Martin Luther King Jr. based his Albany work there [src: 1, src: 4]. Working that office phone, she once dialed Harry Belafonte's number at King's request [src: 1]. Out of the notes she took at mass meetings, sometimes on hand fans when she ran out of paper, she later wrote Open Dem Cells: A Pictorial History of the Albany Movement, published under the name Mary Royal Jenkins by Brentwood Academic Press in 2000 [src: 1, src: 6]. Her oral history was recorded in Albany on March 9, 2013, the same day as fellow Albany narrator Mary Jones (entry 85), and is held by the Library of Congress as entry 84 of the Civil Rights History Project (AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Mary Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling a downtown Albany sidewalk in her childhood, Jenkins describes the lesson her mother enforced under Jim Crow:
interview_snippets.quote: And a little white boy just about my age walked up and squirted a water pistol in my face. And I tried desperately to get away from my mother so that I could catch him. But she kept saying, you can't. And pretty much you can't has followed my life.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mary Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: On her first teaching post, at a school in rural Baker County:
interview_snippets.quote: It was at Baker County, that I learned the power, white power, what white power could do.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mary Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: After a white supervisor assembled the Black teachers to argue against school desegregation, Jenkins recounts how the supervisor closed and how she responded:
interview_snippets.quote: She said they may make us go to school with you. But I would never invite one of you to my home. I was through with Baker County.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mary Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: On what moved her at the first mass meeting and why she joined the Albany Movement:
interview_snippets.quote: I wanted all of that out of my life, not only for myself, but I wanted it out for my children. I wanted them to have a better quality of life than I had as far as segregation was concerned.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mary Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: Explaining how the notes that became her book began, as she listened to people describe their treatment in jail:
interview_snippets.quote: And so I began to just take notes of what some of them were saying. Sometimes I would write on fans, I'd run out of paper in my purse.

movement_context: The Albany Movement Jenkins joined was formed in mid-November 1961 by a coalition of the Ministerial Alliance, the NAACP, the Federation of Women's Clubs, the Negro Voters League, and SNCC; William G. Anderson, the young Black osteopathic physician who is catalog entry 126, was selected as its first president, and SNCC field secretaries Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon had been on the ground in Albany since October 1961 running nonviolence workshops and voter-registration drives, principally among the students at Albany State College where Bernice Johnson Reagon (later a founding Freedom Singer) was then enrolled [src: 3]. The Movement's stated aim was to end segregation across an entire city, in bus terminals, lunch counters, libraries, parks, and the registration rolls, and the first mass meeting in Albany's history convened at Old Mt. Zion Baptist Church on November 17, 1961, the date the New Georgia Encyclopedia treats as the Movement's founding [src: 3, src: 4]. Martin Luther King Jr. and the SCLC entered the campaign in December 1961 at the Movement's invitation; King was arrested December 16 alongside hundreds of others, and the campaign extended through the summer of 1962, producing roughly twelve hundred total arrests [src: 3]. Albany Police Chief Laurie Pritchett's tactical innovation, dispersing arrested protesters into the jails of surrounding counties so the city's own cells never overflowed and the national-press mass-arrest dynamic never built, is the operational detail civil rights historians point to as the conventional-national-policy failure of the campaign [src: 3]. The local outcome was different: the 1963 repeal of Albany's segregation ordinances and a deep cadre of Movement-trained Albany teachers, organizers, and church people (the Sherrods, the Reagons, William G. Anderson, the Freedom Singers, and Jenkins herself in her teaching and documentation roles) carried the work forward across the next half-century [src: 3, src: 5]. Jenkins's own narrative is anchored in the Black-public-school-teacher position the Movement put under direct political stress: her oral history recalls teaching first in rural Baker County, where, in her words, she "learned the power, white power, what white power could do," and then at Carver Junior High School in Albany [src: 1]. At the Baker County school, a white supervisor assembled the Black teachers to argue against the Supreme Court's school-desegregation ruling and closed with the line "they may make us go to school with you, but I would never invite one of you to my home"; that was the moment, Jenkins says, she "was through with Baker County" [src: 1]. Baker County's reputation among southwest-Georgia organizers for white violence and resistance to Black voter registration is documented in the broader Albany Movement record [src: 4]. Her oral history also preserves the office side of the Albany Movement that the SNCC and SCLC field literature less frequently captures: she volunteered in the office King ran from William G. Anderson's house, answered its phone, and on one occasion dialed Harry Belafonte's number at King's request, reaching a message rather than the singer himself, and she took notes on hand fans at mass meetings when she ran out of paper [src: 1].
legacy_and_reception: Jenkins published Open Dem Cells: A Pictorial History of the Albany Movement through Brentwood Academic Press in 2000, a photograph-and-document compilation drawing on her own movement-period notes, the Movement-period Black-press coverage in the Southwest Georgian, and the surviving cardboard-church-fan annotations she had carried out of the mass-meeting period [src: 6]. The book is one of the few first-person Albany Movement reference works produced by a participant who was not herself an organizational principal, and it predates the institutional consolidation of the Albany Movement story at the Albany Civil Rights Institute, which she helped establish [src: 5]. The Institute opened in November 1998 as the Albany Civil Rights Movement Museum at Old Mt. Zion Baptist Church, the same building where the first 1961 mass meeting had been held, and relocated in 2008 to the adjacent twelve-thousand-square-foot facility funded by a 1994 Albany sales-tax allocation of $750,000 [src: 5]. Jenkins remained active with the Albany Dream Awards, the annual recognitions given at the city's Martin Luther King Jr. observance, through the end of her life; she died Thursday, August 7, 2014, five days before what would have been her eighty-sixth birthday on August 12, and a memorial program at the Civil Rights Institute marked the date [src: 7]. Her oral history was recorded in Albany on March 9, 2013, by Will Griffin for the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, less than eighteen months before her death, making the Civil Rights History Project transcript the longest and last documented first-person account she gave on the record [src: 1].

gallery.alt: Albany Civil Rights Institute building exterior, Albany, Georgia
gallery.caption: The Albany Civil Rights Institute, the institution Jenkins helped establish, photographed in 2014. The twelve-thousand-square-foot facility opened on its current Whitney Avenue site in 2008, adjacent to the Old Mt. Zion Baptist Church where the first Albany Movement mass meeting was held on November 17, 1961.

gallery.alt: Georgia Civil Rights Trail historical marker for the Albany Movement, Albany, Georgia
gallery.caption: Georgia Civil Rights Trail marker commemorating the Albany Movement, in Dougherty County. The marker traces the coalition Jenkins joined: SNCC field workers Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon (arrived October 1961), the founding mass meeting at Old Mt. Zion on November 17, 1961, William G. Anderson as first president, and the SCLC entry in December 1961.

gallery.alt: Charles M. Sherrod Civil Rights Park, Albany, Georgia
gallery.caption: Charles M. Sherrod Civil Rights Park in Albany, named for the SNCC field secretary whose 1961 arrival in Albany triggered the local organizing that drew Jenkins and other Albany Black teachers, ministers, and students into the Albany Movement.
